narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 63.4% chance that you would not pass. This prediction stems from a careful look at key elements of your profile, like your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, LSAT score of 28.50, Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings of 2 and 1 respectively, upper-middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. The model flagged your relatively low class decile in Year 3 as the strongest signal pushing toward a failure prediction, since it sits well below the average of around 6 for students who passed the bar. Your Year 1 decile of 2 also contributed notably in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about early academic performance compared to successful peers. On a more positive note, your upper-middle family income level worked in your favor, aligning with the majority of bar passers and slightly pulling the prediction toward success. Overall, these top factors—especially the Year 3 decile—outweighed strengths like your full-time commitment, which is common among those who pass. When compared to typical accepted students, your LSAT and GPA are noticeably lower than their averages of 37.27 and 3.24, and your deciles are in the bottom range versus their mid-to-high placements. This combination made the model view your bar exam odds as riskier than for most admits. While your background as an Asian female pursuing full-time studies didn't factor into the prediction, it highlights your determination in a competitive field. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, and focus on strategies to improve your decile rankings in any ongoing undergrad or prep courses. Building a stronger academic track record now could shift future predictions in your favor. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by targeting these areas—reach out to advisors for personalized prep plans, and reapply once you've strengthened your profile. Your path to law school is still very much open with targeted improvements.
